comments.title: The Kybalion

I didn't know much about it but the book kind of "spoke to me" and I felt an urge to buy it.  I haven't yet read much of it.  As far as serious occult works go, is this one?  Is it worth a study?

replies.title: Re: The Kybalion
replies.content: You've just picked up one of the classic texts of American occultism. The "three initiates" who wrote it are all one person, William Walker Atkinson, of whom I've written here already. It was required reading in a number of magical orders, including one of the ones in which I was initiated, and deserves close study.

comments.title: Dion Fortune, Franz Bardon, Clairvoyance, Rose Cross Ritual, Tarot Court Cards
comments.content:
Dear J.M.Greer,
1) In case you weren't aware of it, and for the benefit of fellow students, I wanted to let you know that Dion Fortune's Rite of the Master Jesus and Guild of the Master Jesus teaching papers, meditations, etc. have been made freely available by the Companions of Dion Fortune here: https://www.dionfortune.co.uk/dion-fortune-grail-papers/the-rite-of-the-master-jesus

Perhaps this material could be adapted for self-initiation into its use. It would be delightful to see this version of the Christ mysteries become more widespread.
The site also has Fortune's Tor Meditation, Chant of the Elements, etc. Of interest to students of the Cosmic Doctrine, here is her 1923 Notes on the Planes & Evolution:
https://www.dionfortune.co.uk/dnfpapers/notes-on-the-planes-and-evolution

Another site of interest to fellow students is Czech Hermetics,
publishers of rare materials of Franz Bardon and translations of other early 20th century Central European occultists, such as Pierre de Lasenic and Theofanus Abba. They have some fascinating articles and downloads.
http://czechhermetics.com/

2) Some fellow students and I like to practice clairvoyance by reading unknown targets and getting feedback on our accuracy. I mentioned such a reading, including accurate details of her personal life, to one of these targets, who brought up the question: is there any way to prevent someone from clairvoyantly perceiving such information about oneself? There was no way known to us. It seemed to us that whatever you do leaves indelible astral traces, or imprints in the anima mundi, or in the akasha, or suchlike, so just as with the Internet, there's no such thing as astral privacy. Is that correct? Perhaps there are temporary methods to hide oneself for a short duration - could the Rose Cross Ritual create such a temporary barrier, or some other method?

3) If I'm not mistaken, you mentioned somewhere on this blog that you did the Rose Cross Ritual daily for a year. What were the effects or benefits of such a rigorous practice of that ritual?

4) What's your recommended approach to understanding the court cards of Tarot? For example, the suits of the Minor Arcana can be approached via their elemental correspondences, the pip cards through numerology - but what about the pages, knights, queens, and kings?

Regards,
Josh Rout

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune, Franz Bardon, Clairvoyance, Rose Cross Ritual, Tarot Court Cards
replies.content:
1) I'm glad the Fortune site has gotten the GMJ material posted. It's a good solid site -- not a lot of conversation there at the moment, but with any luck that'll change. Thanks also for the reference to Czech Hermetics -- that's good to know.

2) There's no such thing as astral privacy. All those internet memes about Ceiling Cat can be applied here:
[IMAGE:0]
Practicing the R+C ritual a lot will help veil your presence from people who don't have a lot of experience, but -- well, this is why mystics and adepts tend to be much more cautious about what they do even in apparent privacy...

3) Hmm. I don't like to talk about my own spiritual experiences, so we'll just say that it helped catalyze several such experiences and I highly recommend it.

4) I work with them as combined elements, like the tattwas -- fire of fire, water of fire, air of fire, and earth of fire, and so on.

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune, Franz Bardon, Clairvoyance, Rose Cross Ritual, Tarot Court Cards
replies.content:
2) Ah, I thought so. For those unused to it, it must be like suddenly waking up to the fact that you're living in an astral nudist colony. At some point I realized we're swimming in a 'sea of minds,' of feelings and thoughts, not all of them of human origin, and we're constantly being influenced by them - even the allegedly non-clairvoyant. So I decided that's another good reason to manage my own thoughts and feelings more carefully - they broadcast all the time and don't just affect me!

3) That's good to hear! I should have said my main concern would be if there are any drawbacks, as with excessive hand washing. Some have likened the effects to invisibility, but the way I experienced it was that it rather changes one's 'frequency,' so merely as a side effect, one would be less apparent to those not attuned to its region of the astral 'spectrum,' or perhaps its spiritual current. Comparable to incidental camouflage, then, rather than invisibility, but that was just how I perceived it.

4) thank you for this suggestion! So the preferred set of correspondences would be pages assigned to air, knights --> fire, queens --> water, kings --> earth?

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune, Franz Bardon, Clairvoyance, Rose Cross Ritual, Tarot Court Cards
replies.content: 4) In the version of the Golden Dawn system I use, no, it's kings = fire, queens = water, knights = air, pages = earth. Of course there are variant systems...

replies.content:

comments.title: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
comments.content:
Hello Mr. Greer,

I have been thinking a lot about your ideas concerning Faustian, Sobernost, and Tantamos culture. You have said that American culture west of the Appalchan mountains is far more individualistic than the 400 year old north east power block. That got me thinking about how America has a Faustian and a Tantamos legal tradition. The Faustian way says that the foundation of American law is the constitution, whereas the Tantamos says it is the declaration of independence. One says, "here is the document that all must follow". The other says, "the only reason why you have to listen to this document or any other legal document is because it starts by declaring your rights are above our legal authority and it is our responsibility to respect those rights". One legal tradition roots our rights in what the government grants while the other roots the government in one individual rights grant. In many ways, this is also the battle between the left and the right, and why the two sides cannot understand the other.

So anyway, given all this I am wondering why we have to wait 500 years or so before America finds that Native American individualism. Isn't it already here in the declaration of independence? Didn't Trump reignite that passion amidst much of flyover America? Isn't the spirit of the changer moving us in that direction right now? Why would it take so long for that kind of thinking to metastasize when it looks like the thin veneer of Faustian culture is quickly fading in most areas accept a few hold outs? I would have thought that at the rate things are going it would only take several more generations to get there, but you seem quite confident that this is a fairly distant development.

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content:
Oh, we'll definitely see movement in that direction -- as you note, we're seeing it now -- but the Declaration of Independence should be seen as a first step toward the future Tamanous culture, not an actual manifestation of it. The current movements toward a vision of human possibility rooted in individual autonomy will go a certain distance, then there'll be a movement back in the other direction (which will allow the vision to gain strength and definition through opposition), then another movement, and so on.

No doubt people in each of the forward movements will think they've arrived at the Promised Land, but here's the crucial point: nobody raised in our present culture can imagine what the future Tamanous culture will look like. Imagine an inhabitant of Roman Britain trying to figure out what English civilization circa 1700 was like -- he'd be completely incapable of getting even the rough outline. We have a long strange journey ahead of us, and there'll be some very rough patches on the way.

None of that should be taken as any kind of discouragement for those who are setting out on the journey now! There are potentials wide as the sky open at this moment, for those who can shake off the Faustian pseudomorphosis and its obsessive-compulsive managerial state.

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content:
The Tamanous was probably my favorite part of your book, "The King in Orange." And I loved most of the book and found it very informative. But I haven't been able to forget that aspect of it, and keep thinking quite a bit about the Tamanous. I've decided to take that as a sign that I should set out on the journey now. Given how individualized the Tamanous is for each person, I'm not really worried about "getting it right," and mostly wish to avoid anything dangerous.

Last week, I mentioned that I was going to be taking a dive in winter water soon. I plan to incorporate the ritual that I'm developing into my own Tamanous spirituality. I think I know how to do it now. It is simple but I think it'll be effective. I plan to make a general confession to a priest a few hours before I'm scheduled to take the plunge. Right before the plunge, I'll say an act of contrition. Once out of the water and warmed up, I will drive to a nearby parish and spend time in Eucharistic Adoration, then ask the folk saint, Fr. J.M. Villars, to be my guardian Tamanous.

Of course, the part about me not being too worried about getting it right shouldn't be taken too literally. Do you think this sounds like a workable plan? Since there's no Tamanous American culture, yet, I'm just taking the things I know work and fusing together my own action plan.

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content: That sounds like an entirely sensible approach. I don't claim to know enough about Catholicism to judge, but from within the traditions I know well, that sequence would be a classic way to enter into a deeper level of spiritual practice.

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content:
Sounds good to me!

If I may add a recommendation for the months ahead, look up the life of St. John Bosco, saint patron of apprentices, the youth in general and juvenile delinquents in particular and, ahem, magicians. As for what do this guy has to do with the Tamanous culture...

I am quoting from memory for a book I read more than 20 years ago, Vivieron el Evangelio (They lived the Gospel), but IIRC: "Saint John Bosco was the living proof that Christ does not cast you into a mold, but compel you to strive for sainthood in the way closest to your natural dispositions". The man certainly made a ruckus wherever he went. SJB was an early vocation; at age 9 he used performance arts,- such as juggling and simple (stage) magic tricks.- to draw the attention of the neighbors and then preaching last Sunday's sermon to them. As a priest, he started working as a chaplain in jail, but decided that as much as these men needed consolation, his time was better spent reaching out to disadvantaged youths before they wrecked their lives. His ministry earned him more than a couple enemies. At some point, the bishop of his diocesis tried to put him in an asylum for the insane, but SJB conned the aides into locking up the Bishop's vicar instead.

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content: Thanks for the suggestion and also for the interesting (and amusing!) anecdotes from the life of St. John Bosco!

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content: Do you think there is any chance that at some point our species will exhaust all of its creative options for cultural expression?  If so, is that about the time when a conscious, self aware species has run its course, dies out, and clears the way for another type of conscious expression through another species?

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content: Of course. Humanity is itself one finite subset of the possibilities of the cosmos, and it'll take a while before we work through all the options but eventually we'll do so. I've sometimes thought of writing a story set very late in our species' life cycle, where everything has been done countless times and culture is a matter of tending the relics of the multimillennial past; John Brunner did something not unrelated in his SF novel Catch a Falling Star, but I'd make it more central.

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content: I'd definitely read that story.

replies.title: Re: Faustian Culture and the Foundation of American Law and Culture
replies.content:
In addition to influences from Native American law and governance (mainly the NE tribes known to Founders), there is a residue of Spanish and French law codes in the South (Louisiana) and Southwest. For instance, the concept of community property in marriage vs. male owns all concept from English Common Law.

Rita

comments.title: The Astral vs. VR
comments.content:
What is the relationship, if any, between awareness on the astral plane of imagination and the mind-state generated by participation in any kind of techno-generated virtual reality environment?

I’ve just been watching Zuckerberg’s presentation of his “meta” VR system; instead of creating an internet of things, it brings his virtual world illusionistically into the real world through various devices; it’s cell phones on steroids. I don’t like it, and I have a hunch you won’t like it too; but I think the odds are good a lot of kids will go for this. What are the implications, magically and imaginatively?

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content: It's a crutch. If a bunch of kids grew up using crutches all the time, they'd probably never be very good at walking, and the same is likely true of the imagination. I hope it turns out to be as deadly dull as it sounds.

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content:
A tangentially related anecdote on imagination. A friend of mine has a young son, and at the time of this anecdote (a couple years ago, when the kid was five)), he and his wife hadn't let him watch TV. At all. My friend told me his son would tell very, elaborate, long 'stories' with detailed and elaborate descriptions of what he had done that day at school, with different people, things, etc., etc. Then the kid would laugh, and say he made it up. He didn't know whether this was because they didn't let him watch ANY TV (if all 5 year olds would do this without TV) or if his son had a special talent in this area.

When my friend told me this, it moved him rather deeply, a side of him I hadn't seen before. His kid's storytelling seemed to amaze him.

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content: Most five year olds will do this if their imaginations weren't stunted by TV. Encourage him to keep the kid telling stories -- it's an ability that can be cultivated.

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content: I've wondered for a while, given that our society is fixated in materializing every aspect of human beings so it can cash on them, if the internet resembles the lower astral plane in at least some sense. I've heard it is pretty muddy too, 80% of it is pornography after all... We do access it using a black "scrying mirror" too.

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content: That's a very useful way to look at it.

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content: And suddenly the weird way that serious TV addicts never seem to have any original ideas just started to make a lot more sense!

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content: Doesn't it, though...

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content: If its any consolation my 21 and 19 year old think Meta is the dumbest thing they've ever seen and see it as designed for "the olds".

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content: I hope that this is true across the board. I've suspected since the covid thing started that barring a major shock, the near future will see the younger generations walk away from socially acceptable lifestyles and start using a lot simpler technology, while a large fraction of the older generations will retreat ever further into a technologically mediated stupor. The ensuing generational conflicts and divergent worldviews will make what we've had thus far look very tame...

replies.title: Re: The Astral vs. VR
replies.content:
Their generation uses online media in a very ironic way. Nothing they say or do online is without irony and a lot of sarcasm. Most of it looks very bland to those outside their generation but it has layers of meaning to them based on very small in-groups. There are few in that generation that are fans of any of the corporate driven art and entertainment. They assume they are being lied to by everyone outside their generation and everyone says things to manipulate others. It's a cynical view of life but it is so so true.

So no, I'm not worried about Gen Z. The Millenials though (ages 25-45 or so) are a different story when it comes to media, Meta, etc.

A couple of ideas I hope might be useful:

1) Building on JMG's 3), I just recalled a diffused attention exercise I worked out on my own as a form of meditation back in high school. I would sit in the shower with the water flowing and close my eyes. First, I would pick out one sound (say, the water hitting the floor of the shower) and notice it distinct from the other sounds. Then I would pick another (say, the water going down the drain), and try to pay attention to both at the same time. I'd keep this up for as many different distinct sounds as I could, trying to hear all of them with equal attention. Then I would try to do the same thing for physical feelings (the water hitting my head, running down my body, the cold or warmth of the floor).

(Given how long I would sometimes do this, I now realize this was extremely wasteful, so I can't fully recommend it, but I imagine any situation with multiple modes of sensory stimulation simultaneously could work - maybe sitting in a rainstorm? Or on a breezy and sunny day with birdsong and leaves rustling)

2) I don't remember if I mentioned this before, but Drawing on the Right Side of the Brain by Betty Edwards is a) a fantastic book for learning to draw, and b) is entirely focused on the shift in awareness/state of consciousness needed to draw well. It's not exactly "diffuse", but it is very different from the kind of verbal, analytical, symbol-manipulating thinking that is the default for very many of us, and the first few times her exercises have you notice it happen, it's pretty striking.

As I said, not sure if either of these is what you're looking for, but I hope they might point you in some helpful directions.

Cheers,
Jeff

replies.title: Re: Banishing rituals and spirits
replies.content: No, it's a common question, but it's based on a misunderstanding. A banishing ritual isn't spirit repellent! What a banishing ritual does is bring you and the space around you into a state of spiritual balance. Noxious spirits and energies can't stand that, and leave, but helpful spirits and energies are just fine with it. The banishing ritual I mostly teach these days, the Sphere of Protection, specifies what it banishes -- "all harmful influences and hostile magic, and every imbalance of the nature of (element)." Your dead relatives and pets shouldn't find that any inconvenience at all.

replies.title: Re: Banishing rituals and spirits

replies.title: Re: Banishing rituals and spirits
replies.content: Good. Both rituals establish a state of spiritual balance -- that's why they're so nearly identical -- but each one has a secondary focus. The standard Hermetic GD version of the LBRP is meant to draw your consciousness away from the material plane -- that's why it uses the banishing pentagram of Earth -- while that version of the LIRP focuses your consciousness on the material plane. (In the Druidical GD we do things differently.) But those are secondary to the main purpose of establishing balance.

replies.title: Re: Banishing rituals and spirits

replies.title: Re: Banishing rituals and spirits
replies.content: The Druidical GD traces the pentagrams differently; we use the invoking pentagram of Spirit to invoke and the banishing pentagram of Spirit to banish. The same balancing effect is present, but the invoking opens up the inner senses of the practitioner, and the closing closes them back down.

comments.content:
Hi JMG.

A few MM’s ago, I provided some data which elicited a suggestion from you that I might have “the ability to use scent the way clairvoyants use sight”. Clair…olfaction? Anyway, after some further experiences I have come to think this is correct.

replies.title: Re: Clear smelling
replies.content: Clairolfaction is the usual term, yes. I don't think I've ever encountered a discussion of how to train it, so you're breaking new ground; you might read W.E. Butler's book How to Read the Aura, Practice Psychometry, Telepathy, and Clairvoyance and see if you can modify the exercises to work with a different psychic sense.

replies.title: Re: Clear smelling
replies.content:
As somebody in the same boat:

I haven't seen any books dealing with this directly. I did get a great deal out of learning about scent tracking in animals though.

Franz Bardon's Initiation Into Hermetics has an exercise where you try to imagine that you smell something (say, the scent of lavendar), and hold onto it for five minutes at a stretch.

Beyond that, learning how to pay attention to smells without making any obvious sniffing sounds is useful. You'll want to actively pay attention to what you are smelling at different points in the day. From there, the next step is to learn to break down scents and determine what makes them up and what is actually present. This appears to be a simple matter of concentration.

At some point, smells will begin to have a definite sense of shape and presence if they don't already. At that point, trying to smell any particular item will tend to draw additional insights about it.

One word of warning; when I started these practices it also sharpened my memory in ways I found really unpleasant.

replies.title: Re: Clear smelling
replies.content:
Not with any connection to the occult, but something that might help with becoming more aware of taste/smell, which might help sort out the astral versions of same:

Tim Ferriss's "The 4 Hour Chef" is a learn-to-cook cookbook that is actually a case study for "how to learn complex skills efficiently". In the actually-about-cooking section, he includes a set of exercises for learning to taste precisely enough to make decisions while cooking, and of course a large part of that is smell.

The book is certainly interesting on its own merits, but here's a brief summary of the exercise, which I imagine could be adapted to smells pretty well:

1. Smell like a dog - literally get down close to the food and smell deeply (likely best done in private!)
2. Literally deconstruct your food - when learning a recipe, try each ingredient separately, and gradually build up to the full, complex taste. If you're having trouble remembering what a particular spice tastes like (say, cumin), pick a dish that really tastes like it and remember it that way (chili)
3. Leverage non-tongue taste - put some herbs or other things you're trying to learn to smell in hot water - smell the water, swish it around in your mouth, aerate it like someone at a snooty wine-tasting, and then swallow it
4. Isolate the basics - pick something that is pretty much only one of the flavor basics (like sugar for "sweet", or lemon juice for "sour", table salt for "salty") and do the same with some other qualities like spiciness or astringency
5. Try unusual food combinations - find seemingly weird things that someone work anyway, taste them, and then ask yourself why the work - like cinnamon and chile powder on ice cream or mustard on black eyed peas)

replies.title: Re: Clear smelling
replies.content:
I had a clairaudient/clairolfactant experience once, in which I heard Ann Lander saying "wake up and smell the coffee" at the same time that I smelled coffee. The coffee smell was so intense that I had to get out of bed and look at the kitchen stove to convince myself that my husband wasn't making coffee. If you are old enough to have read Ann Lander's advice column you'll know what the saying refers too, and it turned out I was getting a warning that a meeting which was to occur the next day would not go well.

In that respect it might be interesting to note that when I was in elementary school playing in the school band I taught myself how to isolate the sounds of particular instruments out of the sound-experience of an entire ensemble. And as a chemist I learned how to identify quite a few different chemicals by smell.

2) You wrote in a recent MM about making your life a “single gesture of will”. I think I understand, but may I trouble you to say a bit more about this, and how one goes about doing this?

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
1) I don't see them as archetypes so much as a useful reframing of Freud's categories of superego, ego, and id. The archetypes Jung discusses are by and large much more focused and specific than the three broad categories Berne and the transactional analysts worked with.

2) Most people fail to accomplish much of anything in their lives because their wills are divided, so they're trying to accomplish conflicting goals at the same time. Consider the person who wants to get rich and also wants to satisfy various expensive desires -- you can't do both! If you can sort out your desires and wishes and wants, decide which one of them is the most important and which others are compatible with it, and then make every decision at every moment on the basis of whether it furthers (or at least does not conflict with) your primary goal, on the other hand, you can accomplish astounding things. That's what I mean by making your life a single gesture of will. If all your actions move toward a single coherent purpose, you can accomplish pretty much whatever you desire.

replies.title: Re: CGD Ovate temple work
replies.content:
1) Perfection is not required. My first set, when I was developing the system, was snipped out of colored paper, and they weren't anything close to perfect.

2) Further meditation is the best approach. Spend some time meditating on something else, and then go back to it.

3) As often as divination suggests.

4) The Hermetic GD's version of the LBRP, which banishes the element of earth, isn't good to do outdoors, since the earth elementals and other earth-related spirits quite reasonably find that unfriendly. The Druidical GD's version doesn't have that problem, since it doesn't banish the earth energies. By all means tell the spirits in the place what you're going to do, and use divination to make sure it's okay with them; you might also make a small offering of some suitable kind to the nature spirits before you begin.

Excalibur has an important part in Druid ritual since way back. Here's the unsheathing of the sword at the Welsh Gorsedd of Bards:
[IMAGE:0]

Is that the real, historical Excalibur? WAS there a real, historical Excalibur?

replies.title: Re: Grove & Sword
replies.content:
No, it's the ceremonial sword of the Gorsedd y Beirdd -- the Welsh national organization that supervises bardic contests and the traditional poetry. It wqas made in the 19th century. Was there a real historical Excalibur? Well, the Roman-British warlord whose memory comes down to us in the legends of King Arthur will certainly have had a sword -- that was the standard military sidearm in those days.

On the other hand, the whole business of "drawing the sword from the stone" probably dates back to far more ancient times, when extracting iron for swords from stony ore was a secret of kings. A lot of very ancient lore ended up being swept up in the Arthurian legends!

comments.title: occultism and spirituality
comments.content:
What I find compelling about your occult philosophy, JMG, is the rich network of correspondences and relationships that imbue even the most mundane aspects of the world with deeper meaning. Your new year's lesson on numerology is a case in point.

It's a sharp contrast with the materialist-utilitarian worldview of my upbringing that sees the world, and life, as "just one darned thing after another." If I read you correctly, this is the worldview of sleepwalking people, people entranced.

My question is open-ended: How is the occult perception of rich correspondences among phenomena related to personal spiritual awakening? Is this a chicken-and-egg problem?

replies.title: Re: occultism and spirituality
replies.content: No, it's not a chicken-and-egg issue, it's a chicken-all-by-itself issue. The capacity to experience the world in terms of a network of meaningful correspondences is an essential part -- perhaps the essential part -- of the mode of spiritual awakening pursued and practiced in occultism. If you pursue the one you pursue the other, if you have the one you have the other. As for how to get there, why, that's what occult study and practice are about!

replies.title: Re: occultism and spirituality
replies.content: I'm going to start thinking about this as The Path of the Fractal Chicken as the western equivalent of Indra's net. Hehe

replies.title: Re: occultism and spirituality
replies.content: "the Fractal Chickens" sounds like a really outré Sixties acid rock band.

replies.title: Re: occultism and spirituality
replies.content:
Within materialism, complex systems science can also have this effect on some people. Other parts of the materialist system are like this as well, but are generally less accessible or don't as readily imply meaningful connections with psychology or the human condition.

As sciences, these fields are not usually taught as part of a complete system of practice. This leads to the question: is it dangerous to the audience to present these fields in ways that could cause some of them to "self-initiate" into these ways of thinking? I would have thought that an intensive practice of contemplating these correspondences, without a corresponding balancing practice or source of grounding, would make someone psychologically vulnerable. But my weak impression from limited anecdata is that, at least for people who stay close to the science, "shallow" and "deep" students don't differ much in how often they have e.g. manic or psychotic episodes, neither kind has them very frequently, and those that do are often the ones with underlying susceptibilities. Nor do people who study complex systems science seem to become cranks noticeably more often than people who study other sciences. Other than that, when there are problems, mostly they seem to come when someone has personality imbalances that warp their speculations in the service of a psychological goal, and when they go out on a limb away from the known science, where there are fewer correspondences and analogies to restrain biased speculation from snowballing on its own extrapolations. Sometimes the field of study itself contains correspondences for this kind of snowballing process, with implications for how to recognize or avoid it, and this may offer some protection--my anecdata are equivocal.

My biggest uncertainty right now is that maybe this kind of training within materialism makes people prone to overconfident predictions that, if they engage in shiny meaning-dense practices taken from occultism proper, the consequences will be limited.

replies.title: Re: occultism and spirituality
replies.content: Interesting. I found systems theory very intuitive when I came to it after some years of exposure to occultism, for what it's worth.

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune
replies.content:
I look forward to hearing JMG's thoughts on the subject, but as far as I know:

Prior to the rise of the theosophical society, the two main diagrams used to map out both the man and the universe were Ptolemaic astronomy or the rose-cross emblem, usually containing a 22-petal rose with letters on them. The only alternative I've seen from that period is Alchemy.

The Theosophical Society was the first public occult organization of the modern world, and it was VERY successful for a time. One of the quirks of that system is that they use seven as a pattern number and take it much more seriously than any other system I'm familiar with. A side effect of this is that a lot of older magical material, and new systems that arose afterwards, insisted on shoe-horning everything into a sevenfold symbolism. I believe this is what DF was talking about.

Regarding differences in lists, correspondences do get reworked sometimes and for a variety of reasons. On top of this, the underlying Jewish theurgical traditions that gave rise to the Cabala are surprisingly diverse. If different source material is used, differences are inevitable. As a final layer, there are similar and parallel traditions of Christian Cabala and some of the more mystical material from the Orthodox church.

Hopefully this is helpful. Cheers!

replies.title: Re: Dion Fortune
replies.content:
1) Fortune lived at a time when Theosophy was still the dominant influence in British occultism, and the distinctive sevenfold Theosophical set of correspondences, based on the seven rays, had become standard. That's what was in the space where the Qabalah had been.

2) It's partly from Regardie's The Golden Dawn and partly from the collected inner experiences of the three authors of LRM.

comments.title: Epiphany Water and House Blessing
comments.content:
This is not a question, but something I thought would be of interest.

In liturgical Christian practice (more Orthodox historically, but adopted by western Catholics more recently), the Epiphany (12 days after Christmas) is associated with the baptism of Christ. There's a specific ritual for blessing water on the Eve of Epiphany, and then it is customary to take the blessed water home, along with blessed chalk, and sprinkle every room in the house, and then make the mark of the Magi (this year, 20 + C + M + B + 22) over the door lintel with blessed chalk.

The blessing of water is a nice bit of liturgy (AKA ritual) and I thought might be of interest.
https://archive.ccwatershed.org/media/pdfs/21/01/04/18-30-10_0.pdf

SamChevre

replies.title: Re: Epiphany Water and House Blessing
replies.content:
The Orthodox version of this is that at Epiphany, you bless water-- theoretically, it's when you make all the holy water for the year. Everybody takes some home (I think the chalk is a Catholic thing), and everybody makes arrangements to have the priest come out and do a house blessing in the following weeks, which is basically an exorcism with sprinkling of holy water in every room of the house.

Some churches, if there's a convenient body of water nearby, will march down and bless the nearest body of water also. And in warmer climes we like to throw a cross in the water and let all the young fellows dive for it. In Russia and Alaska, which are full of crazy people, there is some related epiphany activity that involves chopping a hole in the ice and taking a quick dip.

comments.title: Redeeming Witchcraft
comments.content:
Dear JMG, et al:

My question concerns trad Wicca, which I know isn't your cup of tea, but hear me out. I'm not a Wiccan or Witch (trad/eclectic/folkloric/etc.), yet ever since I started seriously researching Western esotericism over a decade ago I've always had some fondness for trad Wicca, especially when it leans more towards Western occultism than to popular Neopaganism. I love that it's "the Golden Dawn taken outdoors" or "Freemasonry for hippies." However, I'm disturbed by many Witches involvement with malefic magic and demonolatry in recent years. In response to this I know of at least one tradition, Avalonian Druidcraft, which draws its inspiration and magical ethics from "white witchcraft" as opposed to the black magic so popular these days. Yet, what could trad wiccans do to keep the "dark arts" and the above mentioned dak archetype out of the Craft so that it might survive as a viable path of Pagan ceremonial and natural magic? I invite your Wiccan readers as well to offer suggestions. Happy New Year to all.

Sincerely,
Anon.

replies.title: Re: Redeeming Witchcraft
replies.content: I'll have to leave this for the Wiccans in the readership. It's not just that Wicca isn't my cup of tea, it's that I know very little about it. Back in the day, certainly, Wiccans used to dismiss angrily the notion that their Craft had anything to do with malefic magic -- and I've also watched the Neopagan scene plunge into demonolatry and malefic magic in recent years as though none of those dismissals meant anything at all. It's been a ghastly spectacle to witness. But you'd have to talk to somebody who knows more about Wicca than I do to figure out what went wrong and how it might be prevented.

replies.title: Re: Redeeming Witchcraft
replies.content:
Not a Wiccan, and it's been rather a long time since I read my first girlfriend's Starhawk books and almost as long since I read Drawing Down the Moon in college, so please take all this as very tentative and speculative!

I remember that (one of) the core idea(s) was the polarity of the Goddess and the God. Now that I've been reading our host's books on Revival Druidry, it strikes me that this might lead to a lot of focus on binaries, and not on other relationships (like ternaries and quaternaries and so forth). Binaries tend to be about difference, distinction, oppositeness, which at their best get people in mind of complementarities, but at their worst can become all about conflict and us/them and such. I don't know whether being aware of these tendencies would be enough to encourage the healthy side of binaries and discourage the less helpful, but it might be a start.

comments.title: Radioactive pendants
comments.content:
Hi JMG,

I came across this article the other day:

https://arstechnica.com/science/2021/12/anti-5g-quantum-pendants-are-radioactive/

In spite of the dismissive tone, I found it quite interesting - while I would not wish to speculate on the health benefits or otherwise of the pendants, and it's possible that the radiation risk is simply an excuse to get the things banned by the authorities, the materials listed which the pendants are made from are indeed mildly radioactive and I have also noted on here in the past that many holy sites have some aspect of radiation to them. I'm thinking of phospatic chalk in the area round Stonehenge, monoliths made from radon-releasing granite, geologically active areas and hot springs where radioactive rocks are closer to the surface, and even mountains and high places, which have greater radiation levels than at sea level.

My only hope is that all the radioactive material that humans have mined will give future humans some greater connection to the unseen (possibly in the far-off future, once radiation levels have dropped to less fatal levels, or humans gave evolved greater radiation tolerance).

replies.title: Re: Radioactive pendants
replies.content:
It tells me that the people who are making the pendants know what they're up to, in at least some sense. They're convinced that the presence of ions can block the harmful effect of electromagnetic radiation -- and despite the clueless nature of the essay, EMR can certainly have harmful effects -- and so they're using substances that emit a small but steady stream of ionizing radiation to generate the ions. I'm not at all sure this is a good idea -- it sounds to me like swapping one problem for another -- but the principle is at least understandable.

And you're right that modest amounts of radiation do seem to be associated with certain kinds of spiritual experience. So there's definitely more research to be done.

comments.title: A few questions...
comments.content:
Hello, it's my first time asking questions here on Magic Monday. : )

1) Can clairaudience occur when asleep? I had a startling experience last year, I was asleep, not exactly dreaming, it was just an audio experience, no visual whatsoever, just completely black, the entire focus was on what I was hearing and I heard my dad's voice as clear as a phone call, in fact that's what it felt like. This was about 6-12 months after my dad had passed away. I woke up feeling very emotional and overwhelmed but comforted at the same time that he had said hello. There wasn't any particular message, all I remember was him asking if I could hear him, he sounded very excited to get through to me. I will never forget it, it was intense and real.

2) Can tinnitus be something more than just a problem? I've had tinnitus for 8+ years, in just my right ear, a low beeping sound, unknown cause, perfect hearing. It was bothersome at first, especially when trying to sleep. I've had all the tests, scans etc. Tried acupuncture, biofeedback. I also went on a tinnitus clinical trial for a neuromodulation device for 6 months, that did have an effect after a few months, it went away for 2 years, then came back again but not as loud. I've pretty much accepted it now, it's not so bad. Recently I've had some interesting sounds in my left ear, like the sound of a lightsaber, low pitch and volume, not unpleasant and mostly only noticeable at night when I go to bed. Can what I'm hearing be anything other than just tinnitus?

3) Have you heard of Castle Matrix in Ireland? or Sean O'Driscoll (also known as Colonel John Joffre Driscoll) now deceased. Do you know of the Fellowship of Isis? (all linked)

4) I have done quite a lot of divination in various forms and I'm keen to try Ogham but I'm not sure where to start, I have been reading and studying more about the Ogham language recently but I only discovered on here that it was used for divination. There are quite a few standing stones in my part of the world and I've always been intrigued by the marks. Is Ogham divination done by the symbols on cards or sticks?

P.S. I'm really enjoying the numerology, my birth number is 11, so I'm interested to hear a bit more about that. Thanks in advance! Sorry if that was a lot of questions I will try to restrain myself next time! Thanks again, Clare.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Figured I'd throw in my anecdotal experience as someone who has practiced art (visual, poetry, brief stint in music, and now discovering a love of long-form writing) their entire life.

Art is absolutely sentient, in my opinion. I've had the experience of being 'taken' by the work, not in a possessive sense (though I've definitely had experiences where I was terrified, typically when in spiritually low or unconfident places), but rather a palpable sense of dialogue between myself and the canvas / page, a give and take, and sometimes even explicit psycho-emotional demands. Not explicit communications, but just a sense where the work says, 'No, this is how it needs to be, regardless of your wants, silly.' Learning to roll with that and respect it was one of the most important lessons I've had as an artist.

I tend to believe art is magic, and I've had that belief since before I began my interest in the proper occult. I used to describe it to people as simply 'Well, if I write or say the word apple or paint a picture of an apple, you think of what you know to be 'apple,' even though it's explicitly not an apple. If that's not magic, IDK what is.'

Nowadays I expect there is more to it than that, but maybe not? It might be that's enough.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
An additional anecdote. I think our gracious host may find this amusing:

I've tried over and over and over to make digital painting work. I've spent upwards of $3000 on touchscreen tablets, specialized equipment, fancy styli, all of it.

I can do it well, using the lessons and techniques I've learned from a life at the canvas. I can show it to people and get oohs and ahhs and all the facile complimentary nonsense. Objectively, they're pretty good. I can look at digital paintings, and acknowledge that some of them are pretty technically masterful.

They are -never- alive.

Not in the way a material painting can be. I would bet everything short of my soul that you could put a Surface in the hands Caravaggio, Goya, John William Waterhouse, any of the masters of my game and they couldn't do it.

I even went through a period where I refused to do traditional painting for 3 years, thinking if I could just dedicate myself to it and stop returning to the easel, it would come in time. The best I got was a cold, rather disinterested and perfunctory emotionality. It's just NOT there.

When I went back after those three years, I got this ridiculous joyous sensation, almost flirtatious, like 'Where have you been!? I've missed you! Let's do it again ;)' and it was like coming home.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: You can certainly think of magic as sentient if that helps. You can also approach it through the idea that the magical universe is inhabited, there are lots of beings in it, some of them are considerably more intelligent than human beings, and if you approach them as wise elders, they will help you. Either approach can work; I find the approach of treating the cosmos as inhabited by spirits gets me better results, but of course your mileage may vary.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content: For whatever it may be worth, I experience magic as a sentient but impersonal thing, and it seems to me that this impersonal sentience wants to be actively courted by the magician before it will really respond with any enthusiasm--much as one might court a prospecftive lover, or as a wise married person will always be his spouse every day, however long they may have been married.

replies.title: (no subject)
replies.content:
Not JMG, and not exactly what you're asking, but a couple of thoughts in the right neighborhood that might help get you on the path to finding more what you're looking for:

1) In How to Read a Book Adler and Van Doren talk about a process they call "coming to terms". They point out that when you read several authors on the same or related topics, each is likely to have slightly (or wildly!) idiosyncratic uses of terminology for the same or similar concepts. They recommend that you pick or come up with your own term and then "translate" each author's usage into your own term.

2) If you already know this, my apologies: in formal language about definitions, two words get used - denotation and connotation. Denotation is the dictionary meaning of the word, what it "really" means (see below for some discussion on that). Connotation means "what are the cultural, emotional, and other 'fuzzy' associations of a word that color when you use it, but aren't technically part of the definition."

3) Prescriptive and Descriptive grammar/definitions: This is a distinction I learned in Linguistics classes in college. "Prescriptive Grammar" is what you're taught in school, a set of rules about what is correct and what is incorrect. "Descriptive Grammar" is the set of regular patterns and rules that folks follow without realizing it, and which may or may not match formal prescriptive grammar and may or may not even be consciously explainable by the people who speak them. For example, there are regions in the US where it is perfectly well understood by everybody around what "us'd ta could" (used to could) means - in the past I could do that, but now I cannot. I bring this up here, because it's possible that there might be discussion somewhere about "descriptive definitions", but it might be hard to find your way there without the specific phrase "descriptive grammar"

Hope some of this helps!
Jeff

You're 33 minutes before the end.

In "The Art and Practice of Geomancy", you mention that Amissio 'favorable in any matter if loss is what you desire; this includes such things as love and sexuality (losing your heart)." Does Amissio in this case need to be in the 5th house (or 7th), for this to hold?

I got:

2nd Mother: Puella

3rd Niece: Puer

Since judge was Amissio, I thought, 'hey, I'll lose my heart -> she'll say yes', then I learned before asking that she was in a relationship. Oh, OK. Hence, my question. Is there a subtlety I'm missing?

replies.title: Re: Geomancy  question
replies.content:
Er, you can lose your heart and still get turned down!

As for the house question, it depends on whether you're casting the reading as a house chart of a shield chart. If you cast this as a shield chart, it's not favorable -- you met her (Conjunctio), you got your hopes up (Amissio), and away she went (Fortuna Minor.) If you cast it as a house chart, you needed to look for a mode of perfection connecting the 1st and 5th or 1st and 7th houses; there isn't one, so the answer is no.

Note also that Rubeus in the 1st house pretty consistently means you're fooling yourself.

If you want to cast a house chart in the future, yes, the 7th house is lasting relationships, the 5th is short term flings.

comments.title: Why Hermes?
comments.content:
I was speaking to a standard Christian friend the other day and was mentioning about alternate ways people interact with the spiritual side of things.

At one point I mentioned that sometimes when they were in a place where they were open to such things that a deity would bring themselves to their attention and that might inspire them to start worshiping eg Hermes.

Their response was to ask me 'what could Hermes give you that the True God can't? Why would anyone worship Hermes?'

what would be a response to such a question from your perspective?

replies.title: Re: Why Hermes?
replies.content: I probably wouldn't get into the conversation, since the kind of monotheist who starts going on about "the true god" is rarely open to a constructive discussion of that subject. If I had to, I'd start by pointing out that Christianity has no monopoly on miracles, spiritual experiences, or any of the other things that religions provide for their believers; people who pray to other gods get exactly the same benefits that Christians do. That being the case, to claim that one god is "the true god" and all others aren't is dubious logic at best. That doesn't mean that every god is identical to every other god; each god has specific expectations for human believers and a tendency to provide certain benefits more readily than others. So human beings can choose.  Which expectations will you accept, which benefits are important to you? That determines which god you worship.
